one_line_takeaway: Naively appending caller/callee/global context to code models hurts accuracy, but pairing LLM-profiled, relevance-ranked inter-procedural context with explicit reasoning-trace fine-tuning (Qwen2.5-Coder-32B) lifts PRIMEVUL accuracy from a prior best of 55.17% (VulTrial) to 67.78%, a +12.61-point gain over the strongest published baseline.
key_finding: The paper's central empirical thesis is a synergy effect: neither raw inter-procedural context (Table 1) nor profiled-and-selected context alone (Table 8, applied to CodeBERT/UniXcoder) improves -- and often degrades -- accuracy for strong fine-tuned code models; gains only appear when profiled context is paired with explicit structured-reasoning fine-tuning. With the Qwen2.5-Coder-32B backbone, CPRVUL raises accuracy over the strongest function-only baseline (UniXcoder) from 56.65%->67.78% on PRIMEVUL (+11.13 pts), 63.68%->73.76% on TITANVUL (+10.08 pts), and 58.24%->64.94% on CLEANVUL (+6.70 pts). Separately, on PRIMEVUL, CPRVUL's 67.78% accuracy (67.93% F1) exceeds the best previously reported SOTA result, VulTrial at 55.17% accuracy (56.18% F1) -- a +12.61-point / 22.9%-relative accuracy improvement and +10.30-point / 17.9%-relative F1 improvement, also beating LLMxCPG (50.36% acc), Ding et al.'s CoT (51.26% acc), and GPTLens (51.84% acc). Ablations (Table 7, Qwen2.5-32B) show Phase II (reasoning) is the larger contributor: removing it drops accuracy by 15.24 pts (PRIMEVUL), 21.38 pts (TITANVUL), and 12.53 pts (CLEANVUL), while removing Phase I (profiling/selection) drops accuracy by a smaller 2.36 / 1.56 / 2.42 pts respectively. Per-CWE analysis (Table 5, PRIMEVUL+TITANVUL) shows CPRVUL improves accuracy in 28 of 30 CWE/dataset combinations (93.3%), with the largest gains on context-dependent classes such as CWE-264 Access Control (+27.09 pts acc on PRIMEVUL, 52.08%->79.17%) and CWE-200 Sensitive Information Exposure (+23.22 pts, 53.57%->76.79%), but a notable regression on CWE-415 Double Free on TITANVUL (-6.90 pts acc, -11.71 pts F1), attributed to double-free requiring temporal/execution-path reasoning that the current context extraction (callers/callees/globals) does not capture.
core_contribution: CPRVUL, a two-phase context-aware framework for C/C++ source-code vulnerability detection that moves beyond function-level binary classification. Phase I (Context Profiling and Selection) clones the target repository, builds a code property graph (CPG), extracts candidate inter-procedural context (callers, callees, accessed global variables), uses an LLM (GPT-4.1) to generate security-focused profiles for each element and score their relevance, then selects only the highest-impact elements that fit the model's context window. Phase II (Context-Aware Reasoning and Detection) feeds the target function, the selected/profiled context, and auxiliary vulnerability metadata (commit messages, CVE/CWE info, and the vulnerability-fixing diff) into a prompt template (Table 2) that generates structured reasoning traces (observation / security_reasoning / impact / is_vulnerable / confidence_score), which are then used as supervision to LoRA-fine-tune Qwen2.5-Coder (7B and 32B) for reasoning-based detection. The paper also releases context-enriched variants of three existing datasets (PrimeVulCTX, TitanVulCTX, CleanVulCTX) built from 883 repositories, adding 19,858 caller functions, 187,170 callee functions, and 132,633 global variables to 23,904 labeled functions.
threat_model: Defensive/detection setting, not an attacker-adversary threat model: the 'threat' addressed is real-world C/C++ software vulnerabilities (spanning CWE categories such as buffer/memory issues, access control CWE-264, sensitive-information exposure CWE-200, resource management CWE-399, double-free CWE-415, etc.) whose presence and root cause frequently cannot be determined from a single function's code in isolation but depend on inter-procedural context -- how callers invoke the function, what callees do, and shared/global state (illustrated with two real CVE case studies: CVE-2021-46666 in MariaDB's condition-pushdown optimizer, and CVE-2018-58142, an invalid lock release in the Linux USBIP host driver). The paper does not model an active adversary attacking the detection system itself; it targets the problem of a defender/analyst (or automated pipeline) trying to correctly classify a target function as vulnerable or benign given the surrounding codebase.
limitations: (1) The one clearly negative result the paper itself surfaces: CPRVUL underperforms on temporal vulnerability classes, most notably CWE-415 (Double Free), where accuracy drops -6.90 pts on TITANVUL and F1 drops -15.79% on PRIMEVUL relative to baseline -- the authors attribute this to the framework's inter-procedural context (callers/callees/globals) capturing 'spatial' structure but not the temporal/execution-path reasoning double-free bugs require. (2) Scope restricted to C/C++ repositories and to functions of at most 1,024 tokens (a filter imposed to allow fair comparison against UniXcoder's context window), so results may not generalize to longer functions or other languages. (3) The per-CWE analysis (RQ2) excludes CLEANVUL entirely because it lacks CWE annotations for most samples, so its conclusions are drawn only from PRIMEVUL and TITANVUL. (4) The pipeline is heavily dependent on a proprietary LLM (GPT-4.1) for both Phase I context profiling/ranking and Phase II reasoning-trace generation used as fine-tuning supervision -- the paper does not report the cost of this LLM-driven data generation step or ablate profiling-LLM choice. (5) Context integration order (RQ3) is shown to have only a modest effect (1.5-3.2 accuracy points across strategies), indicating the reported gains stem primarily from context selection+reasoning training rather than prompt layout, but this also means the paper does not explore more sophisticated integration architectures. (6) All accuracy comparisons rely on balanced-dataset assumptions; the paper explicitly critiques F1 as potentially misleading in this setting and defends its choice of accuracy as primary metric, but this is a self-selected metric emphasis worth noting when comparing to other papers that lead with F1.

datasets_used: PRIMEVUL (5,508 functions after 1,024-token filter; label validity 86%)
TITANVUL (13,038 functions after filter; label validity 92%)
CLEANVUL (4,348 functions after filter; label validity 91%)

Context-enriched releases: PrimeVulCTX, TitanVulCTX, CleanVulCTX built from 883 repositories, containing 19,858 caller functions, 187,170 callee functions, 132,633 global variables across 23,904 labeled functions
quantitative_results: CPRVUL (Qwen2.5-Coder-32B) accuracy: 67.78% PRIMEVUL, 73.76% TITANVUL, 64.94% CLEANVUL (F1: 67.93%, 74.54%, 63.73% respectively)
Accuracy gain over strongest function-only baseline (UniXcoder), Qwen2.5-32B: +11.13 pts PRIMEVUL (56.65%->67.78%), +10.08 pts TITANVUL (63.68%->73.76%), +6.70 pts CLEANVUL (58.24%->64.94%)
Accuracy gain over prior SOTA on PRIMEVUL (VulTrial, 55.17% acc / 56.18% F1): +12.61 pts accuracy (22.9% relative), +10.30 pts F1 (17.9% relative) -> CPRVUL 67.78% acc / 67.93% F1
Zero-shot GPT-4.1 baseline: ~51% accuracy across all three datasets, precision ~51%, recall 76.74%-83.30% (strong over-flagging bias)
Raw-context injection experiment (Table 1): UniXcoder accuracy on PRIMEVUL drops 56.65%->54.22% when raw caller/callee/global context is concatenated; CodeBERT on CLEANVUL drops 55.67%->52.47%

Phase-I-only (profiled context, no reasoning fine-tuning) experiment (Table 8): does not improve accuracy for CodeBERT or UniXcoder on any of the 3 datasets and often degrades it (e.g., CodeBERT PRIMEVUL 53.21%->52.11%, TITANVUL 54.41%->52.86%)
Ablation (Table 7, Qwen2.5-32B): removing Phase II costs -15.24 pts acc (PRIMEVUL), -21.38 pts (TITANVUL), -12.53 pts (CLEANVUL); removing Phase I costs -2.36, -1.56, -2.42 pts respectively

Per-CWE (Table 5): positive accuracy gains in 28/30 CWE-dataset combinations (93.3%); avg +12.72 pts (PRIMEVUL) and +8.85 pts (TITANVUL); best case CWE-264 Access Control +27.09 pts acc on PRIMEVUL (52.08%->79.17%); worst case CWE-415 Double Free -6.90 pts acc on TITANVUL (68.97%->62.07%)
Context integration strategy (Table 6, RQ3): Insert-After beats Insert-Before/As-Comments by 1.5-3.2 accuracy points across datasets
baselines_compared: LLMxCPG (USENIX Security'25) -- prior SOTA method, cited/compared only, not CPRVUL's own
Ding et al.'s CoT (ICSE'25) -- prior SOTA method, cited/compared only
GPTLens -- prior SOTA method, cited/compared only
VulTrial (ICSE'26) -- prior SOTA method and the best-performing baseline compared, cited/compared only
